items.title: Helicobacter pylori and Gastric Cancer
items.contentHtml:
Helicobacter pylori: Main Risk Factor


Chronic infection by Helicobacter pylori is the most important risk factor for the development of gastric adenocarcinoma, especially the intestinal type of the Lauren Classification. It is estimated that it is responsible for approximately 80% of cases. The infection induces chronic gastritis that can progress to atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia and dysplasia, before becoming cancer. Strains with CagA and VacA genes have greater virulence. The eradication ofH. pyloriin early stages can reduce the risk, although it does not completely eliminate it in the presence of advanced metaplasia.

items.title: Lauren's Histological Classification
items.contentHtml:
Lauren's Ranking: Intestinal vs. Diffuse


The Lauren Classification divides gastric adenocarcinoma into two main types with prognostic and etiological implications. The intestinal type is associated with chronic atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia and Helicobacter pylori infection. It tends to be more differentiated, forms glandular structures and has a better prognosis. The diffuse type is characterized by poorly cohesive cells, diffuse invasion of the gastric wall, no glandular formation and a worse prognosis. It is frequently associated with mutations in the CDH1 (E-cadherin) gene and has a more uniform distribution in age and sex.

items.title: Diffuse Type Gastric Cancer and Signet Ring Cells
items.contentHtml:
Diffuse Gastric Cancer: Signet Ring Cells


The diffuse type of gastric adenocarcinoma, according to Lauren's Classification, is histologically characteristic due to the presence of signet ring cells. These cells accumulate large amounts of intracytoplasmic mucin, displacing the nucleus toward the periphery, giving them the appearance of a ring. This growth pattern results in diffuse infiltration of the gastric wall, without forming cohesive masses or glands. The diffuse type is more aggressive, has a worse prognosis and is related to hereditary gastric cancer syndromes, such as the CDH1 gene mutation.

items.title: Definitive Diagnosis of Gastric Cancer
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis: Endoscopy with Biopsy


The definitive diagnosis of gastric cancer is established by Upper Digestive Endoscopy with multiple biopsies. At least six to eight biopsies should be obtained from the edges and center of any suspicious lesion to ensure sampling of malignant tissue. This technique allows direct visualization of the gastric mucosa, identification of ulcerated, infiltrative or exophytic lesions and obtaining material for histopathological study. Endoscopic ultrasound is useful for local staging (depth of invasion and lymph node involvement), but not for initial histological diagnosis.

items.title: Adenocarcinoma: Predominant Histology
items.contentHtml:
Adenocarcinoma: Most Frequent Histological Type


Gastric adenocarcinoma represents more than 95% of all malignant cancers of the stomach. It originates from the glandular epithelium of the gastric mucosa. Histologically, it can be classified into several subtypes, including tubular, papillary, mucinous, and signet ring cells. Lauren's classification (intestinal and diffuse) is of great clinical and prognostic relevance. Identification of the histological type is essential to guide management and estimate the patient's prognosis, with adenocarcinoma being the main focus in the epidemiology and treatment of gastric cancer.

items.foco: Advanced symptoms: weight loss and anemia
items.title: Symptoms in Advanced Gastric Cancer
items.contentHtml:
Advanced Symptoms: Weight Loss and Anemia


In early stages, gastric cancer is usually asymptomatic or presents non-specific symptoms. However, in advanced stages, the symptoms are more evident and suggestive. Involuntary weight loss and iron deficiency anemia are classic and late manifestations, usually indicating advanced disease. Other symptoms include early satiety, progressive dysphagia if the tumor is in the cardia, persistent vomiting due to pyloric obstruction, and nonspecific epigastric abdominal pain. The presence of these signs should alert us to the need for an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy.

items.foco: Typical metastases: Virchow lymph node, Krukenberg
items.title: Patterns of Gastric Cancer Metastasis
items.contentHtml:
Typical Metastases: Ganglion of Virchow, Krukenberg


Gastric cancer can spread by lymphatic, hematogenous, or peritoneal seeding. Lymphatic metastases are common; Virchow's ganglion (left supraclavicular) is a sign of advanced disease. Peritoneally, implants such as Krukenberg tumor (bilateral ovarian metastasis) or Sister María José nodule (periumbilical) may appear. Peritoneal seeding can also cause malignant ascites. Liver metastases are common through the hematogenous route. These findings indicate an advanced stage and tumor unresectability for curative purposes.

items.title: Curative Treatment: Radical Surgery
items.contentHtml:
Curative Treatment: Gastrectomy and Lymphadenectomy


The only potentially curative treatment for localized gastric cancer is radical surgery, which consists of total or subtotal gastrectomy together with a D2 lymphadenectomy. The extent of the gastrectomy depends on the location and size of the tumor. D2 lymphadenectomy involves the resection of lymph nodes from the perigastric groups and the main vessels of the celiac trunk, improving the prognosis. Perioperative chemotherapy (neoadjuvant and adjuvant) is the standard of care for most patients with resectable stage II-III tumors to optimize oncologic outcomes.

items.foco: Most common location: antrum and lesser curvature
items.title: Anatomical Location of Gastric Cancer
items.contentHtml:
Most Common Location: Antrum and Lesser Curvature


The majority of gastric adenocarcinomas are located in the antrum and lesser curvature of the stomach, representing approximately 50-60% of cases. Other locations include the gastric body (20-30%) and the cardia or gastroesophageal junction (10-20%). The location of the tumor is crucial to determine the type of gastrectomy to be performed (subtotal or total) and the surgical approach. Tumors of the cardia often present with dysphagia, while those of the antrum can cause pyloric obstruction.

items.title: Gastric Preneoplastic Conditions
items.contentHtml:
Gastric Atrophy and Intestinal Metaplasia: Preneoplastic Conditions


Progression towards gastric adenocarcinoma usually follows a sequence of histological changes known as the Correa cascade: chronic active gastritis, chronic atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia and, finally, dysplasia and carcinoma. Intestinal metaplasia, especially the incomplete type, is considered a precursor lesion with high risk. The presence of dysplasia (low or high grade) further increases the risk. In these patients, regular endoscopic surveillance with biopsies is recommended to detect progression and provide early intervention.

items.foco: TNM staging: pillar of prognosis and management
items.title: TNM Staging in Gastric Cancer
items.contentHtml:
TNM Staging: Pillar of Prognosis and Management


The TNM staging system (Tumor, Node, Metastasis) is the fundamental pillar to determine the prognosis and guide the therapeutic management of gastric cancer. The T (Tumor Size) component describes the depth of invasion of the tumor in the gastric wall. The N (Lymph Nodes) evaluates the involvement of regional lymph nodes and their number. The M (Distant Metastasis) indicates the presence or absence of metastases to distant organs. Accurate staging, including computed tomography, PET-CT, and diagnostic laparoscopy, is essential to decide between curative and palliative surgical treatment.

items.title: General Gastric Cancer Screening
items.contentHtml:
General Screening: Not Currently Recommended


General population screening for gastric cancer is not recommended in most Western countries. This is due to the relatively low incidence of the disease, the high associated costs, and the lack of evidence of a significant benefit in reducing mortality in low-risk populations. However, in regions with high incidence, such as Japan and South Korea, screening programs with endoscopy or double contrast radiography are implemented. In other areas, the focus is surveillance of high-risk populations with known preneoplastic conditions.
